text: During the laparoscopic repair of an incisional hernia, the surgeon dissects the peritoneum, pushing the bladder down to create a preperitoneal pocket. A semi-traumatic grasper is used to pull the peritoneum, while a harmonic device aids in separating the adherent scar tissue from the abdominal wall. A polypropylene and polyurethane mesh is prepared to cover the defect, with half placed intraperitoneally and half preperitoneally, including a portion fixed retro pubically on the Cooper ligament.

The surgeon is working on the left side, carefully separating the peritoneum down in the midline where significant scar tissue is present. The previous laparotomy scar reveals visible Prolene sutures. The right Cooper ligament is exposed, and the bladder has been fully displaced downward. Minimal energy is used as the hernia sac is pulled out, and the left Cooper ligament is also visible in the midline, requiring separation due to the hernia's extensive involvement in the scarred tissue.
The surgeon carefully separates the peritoneum from the bladder, effectively pulling down the entire bladder and peritoneum associated with the hernia.

The surgeon has reached the pubic symphysis bone, with the bladder and urethra visible in the middle. The peritoneum is reflected on either side. A mesh is introduced into the pocket, with half placed pre-peritoneal and half intraperitoneal. The mesh is fixed on the Cooper ligament on either side using multiple tackers. The mesh has a blue polyurethane side facing the bowel and a white polypropylene side facing the abdominal wall. Titanium ProTack tackers are used to secure the mesh, ensuring they do not fire over the inferior epigastric vessels.

The surgeon fixes the bladder bitumen together with the mesh, ensuring coverage over the surgical area. Tackers are fired around the mesh, particularly in the middle where the rectus was located, to secure it. The polyurethane material of the mesh minimizes adhesion risk for the exposed portion.
